text: A total proctocolectomy with ileopouch is often performed in patients with ulcerative colitis refractory to medical management. Laparoscopic approaches may provide several benefits but may be technically challenging. Robotic assisted surgeries involving low pelvic dissections continue to gain enthusiasm. The purpose of this video is to demonstrate the key steps in a robotic total proctocolectomy with ileopouch anal anastomosis and loop ileostomy. We present a case of a forty-six-year-old gentleman with a twelve-year history of ulcerative colitis that was refractory to medical management. A colonoscopy revealed multiple pseudopolyps and colitis of his descending colon and rectum. Biopsies did not reveal dysplasia. The patient consented to undergo a robotic total proctocolectomy with J-pouch and diverting loop ileostomy.

A total proctocolectomy with ileopouch is often performed in patients with ulcerative colitis refractory to medical management. Laparoscopic approaches may provide several benefits but may be technically challenging. Robotic assisted surgeries involving low pelvic dissections continue to gain enthusiasm. The purpose of this video is to demonstrate the key steps in a robotic total proctocolectomy with ileopouch anal anastomosis and loop ileostomy.

We present a case of a forty-six-year-old gentleman with a twelve-year history of ulcerative colitis that was refractory to medical management. A colonoscopy revealed multiple pseudopolyps and colitis of his descending colon and rectum. Biopsies did not reveal dysplasia. The patient consented to undergo a robotic total proctocolectomy with J-pouch and diverting loop ileostomy.

The surgical team utilizes a camera port, four robotic ports, and an assistant port for the procedure. The transverse and left colon are mobilized using ports one, two, and three A, while port three B is used for the right colon and pelvic dissection. The ileocolic mesocolon is identified, and the ileocolic vessels are dissected and divided with a robotic vessel sealer. A medial to lateral dissection is performed, retracting the right colon anteriorly, and continuing cephalad until the third portion of the duodenum is visible. The hepatic flexure is mobilized, and the right lateral attachments are divided.

The surgical team utilizes a camera port, four robotic ports, and an assistant port for the procedure. The transverse and left colon are mobilized using port sites one, two, and three A, while port site three B is used for the right colon and pelvic dissection. The ileocolic mesocolon is identified, and the ileocolic vessels are dissected and divided with a robotic vessel sealer. A medial to lateral dissection is performed, retracting the right colon anteriorly, and continuing cephalad until the third portion of the duodenum is visible. The hepatic flexure is then mobilized, and the right lateral attachments are divided.

The surgeon divides the lateral attachments to the cecum, fully mobilizing the right colon. The right ureter is identified and protected as the right mesocolon is divided. Dissection proceeds to the rectosigmoid and left colon, with the peritoneum of the rectosigmoid mesocolon opened for a medial to lateral dissection. The left ureter is identified and kept inferior. The inferior mesenteric artery and vein are divided, followed by division of the left mesocolon up to the ligament of Treitz, continuing dissection to the fourth portion of the duodenum and the pancreas.

The surgeon divides the lateral attachments to the cecum, fully mobilizing the right colon. The right ureter is identified and protected as the right mesocolon is divided. The dissection proceeds to the rectosigmoid and left colon, where the peritoneum of the rectosigmoid mesocolon is opened for a medial to lateral dissection. The left ureter is identified and kept inferior, followed by the division of the inferior mesenteric artery and vein.

The surgeon divides the left mesocolon up to the ligament of Treitz, continuing the dissection towards the fourth portion of the duodenum and the pancreas. The left lateral attachments are divided, and the splenic flexure is mobilized. The dissection follows the medial to lateral plane up to the splenic flexure, with re-identification of the left ureter to prevent injury. The transverse mesocolon is then divided after fully mobilizing the left colon.

The surgeon divides the left lateral attachments and mobilizes the splenic flexure, entering the same medial to lateral dissection plane. The left ureter is re-identified to prevent injury, and the transverse mesocolon is divided after fully mobilizing the left colon.

The surgeon begins the pelvic portion of the operation by dissecting the rectum circumferentially down to the levator muscles. The rectum is divided using a stapler, and the colon is removed through a muscle-splitting extraction site. The J pouch is fashioned extracorporeally, with an anvil inserted before repositioning it in the pelvis. The pouch is inspected for proper orientation, and the anastomosis is completed using an EEA stapler, followed by a leak test. Finally, the diverting ileostomy is completed.

The surgeon begins the pelvic portion of the operation by dissecting the rectum circumferentially down to the levator muscles. The rectum is divided using a stapler, and the colon is removed through a muscle-splitting extraction site for the diverting ileostomy placement. The J pouch is fashioned extracorporeally, an anvil is inserted, and it is positioned in the pelvis, ensuring correct orientation. The anastomosis is completed using an EEA stapler and tested for leaks. Finally, the diverting ileostomy is completed.

The patient tolerated the procedure well and was discharged on post-operative day three. Robotic total proctocolectomy with JPouch and diverting loop ileostomy is effective and provides a short hospital course.
